question.text: A trader expects the spread between near-month and far-month USDINR futures to widen. What calendar spread position should the trader take?

answer.text: Buy far-month futures and sell near-month futures (long calendar spread)
Sell far-month futures and buy near-month futures (short calendar spread)
Buy both near-month and far-month futures to benefit from rising rates
Sell both near-month and far-month futures to profit from falling rates

explanation.text: **The Logic:** If the spread is expected to widen (far month premium increases relative to near month), the trader profits by buying far-month futures (which will gain more) and selling near-month futures. This is a long calendar spread. **The Trap:** Buying or selling both months is directional trading, not spread trading. A calendar spread profits from the change in the price difference between months, not from the absolute direction. **Cross-Exam Context:** Calendar spreads are used across all derivative markets — currency (I Ch5), interest rate (IV Ch5), and equity (VIII Ch5). **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Calendar spreads have lower margin requirements than outright positions because the two legs partially offset each other's risk.
